annotations.label: Axiom

anchor.snippet: is a statement that is taken to be true

annotations.note: The general philosophical notion of an axiom has no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Logical vs non-logical axioms

anchor.snippet: Logical axioms are taken to be true within the system of logic they define and are often shown in symbolic form

annotations.note: Mathlib has no proof calculus distinguishing logical from non-logical axioms.

annotations.label: Classical axiom

anchor.snippet: referred to a self-evident assumption common to many branches of science

annotations.note: A historical/philosophical definition with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Axiom example (equals from equals)

anchor.snippet: When an equal amount is taken from equals, an equal amount results.

annotations.note: This is a congruence property of equality under subtraction, handled by core-Lean substitution rather than a named Mathlib decl.

annotations.label: Postulate

anchor.snippet: Such a hypothesis was termed a postulate

annotations.note: The historical notion of a postulate is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euclid's postulates

anchor.snippet: It is possible to draw a straight line from any point to any other point

annotations.note: Mathlib builds Euclidean geometry on inner-product/affine spaces, not from Euclid's postulates as an axiom system.

annotations.label: Common notion: equal to same thing

anchor.snippet: Things which are equal to the same thing are also equal to one another.

annotations.note: Transitivity of equality is a core-Lean primitive (Eq.trans), not a Mathlib/ declaration.

annotations.label: Common notion: equals added to equals

anchor.snippet: If equals are added to equals, the wholes are equal.

annotations.note: Congruence of addition under equality is automatic by substitution and has no dedicated Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Common notion: equals subtracted from equals

anchor.snippet: If equals are subtracted from equals, the remainders are equal.

annotations.note: Congruence of subtraction under equality is automatic by substitution with no dedicated Mathlib decl.

annotations.label: Common notion: coinciding things

anchor.snippet: Things which coincide with one another are equal to one another.

annotations.note: This is reflexivity/identity of equality, a core-Lean primitive rather than a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Common notion: whole greater than part

anchor.snippet: The whole is greater than the part.

annotations.note: The informal magnitude principle is not formalized as such, though many concrete instances (e.g. strict-subset cardinality) exist.

annotations.label: Set of axioms (modern)

anchor.snippet: a set of axioms is any collection of formally stated assertions

annotations.note: A model-theoretic `Theory` is defined as a set of first-order sentences, i.e. a set of axioms.

annotations.label: Consistency

anchor.snippet: it should be impossible to derive a contradiction from the axioms

annotations.note: Mathlib has the semantic analogue (a theory having a model) but no syntactic notion of deriving a contradiction since there is no proof calculus.

annotations.label: Non-redundancy

anchor.snippet: an assertion that can be deduced from other axioms need not be regarded as an axiom

annotations.note: There is no general Mathlib notion of independence/redundancy of an axiom within a theory.

annotations.label: Gödel's incompleteness (independent statement)

anchor.snippet: to construct a statement whose truth is independent of that set of axioms

annotations.note: Only the Gödel Beta Function Lemma (a stepping stone toward the First Incompleteness Theorem) is present; the incompleteness theorem itself is not proved.

annotations.label: Gödel's second incompleteness theorem

anchor.snippet: the consistency of a theory like Peano arithmetic is an unprovable assertion within the scope of that theory

annotations.note: Mathlib has no proof system for arithmetic and does not formalize the Second Incompleteness Theorem.

annotations.label: Independence of the continuum hypothesis

anchor.snippet: the continuum hypothesis (Cantor) is independent of the Zermelo–Fraenkel axioms

annotations.note: Mathlib mentions (G)CH only in docstrings; it does not formalize forcing or any independence result.

annotations.label: Logical axioms

anchor.snippet: certain formulas in a formal language that are universally valid

annotations.note: Mathlib has no proof calculus with a designated set of logical axioms.

annotations.label: Propositional logic axiom schemata

anchor.snippet: it is common to take as logical axioms all formulae of the following forms

annotations.note: No Hilbert-style propositional axiom schemata are formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Axiom schema

anchor.snippet: a rule for generating an infinite number of axioms

annotations.note: Mathlib theories are plain sets of sentences with no dedicated axiom-schema mechanism.

annotations.label: Completeness of three schemata

anchor.snippet: one can prove all tautologies of the propositional calculus

annotations.note: No propositional proof system or completeness-of-schemata result exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: No pair of schemata sufficient

anchor.snippet: no pair of these schemata is sufficient for proving all tautologies

annotations.note: No such metatheorem about propositional axiom schemata is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Axiom of equality

anchor.snippet: Axiom of equality

annotations.note: Mathlib has no first-order proof calculus containing an equality axiom scheme.

annotations.label: Universal instantiation

anchor.snippet: Axiom scheme for universal instantiation

annotations.note: Universal instantiation as a logical axiom scheme is not formalized; Mathlib has only semantic realization of formulas.

annotations.label: Existential generalization

anchor.snippet: Axiom scheme for existential generalization

annotations.note: Existential generalization as a logical axiom scheme is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-logical axioms

anchor.snippet: Non-logical axioms are formulas that play the role of theory-specific assumptions

annotations.note: A `Theory` is precisely a set of non-logical axioms (theory-specific sentences) of a first-order language.

annotations.label: ZFC set theory

anchor.snippet: the axioms being used are the axioms of Zermelo–Fraenkel set theory with choice, abbreviated ZFC

annotations.note: Mathlib models ZFC via `ZFSet` and derives its axioms (e.g. `ZFSet.choice`) inside type theory, rather than adopting ZFC as a first-order axiom system.

annotations.label: Peano axioms

anchor.snippet: The Peano axioms are the most widely used axiomatization of first-order arithmetic

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Nat` and the weaker Presburger language but no first-order Peano arithmetic theory with its induction schema.

annotations.label: Euclid's 4 + 1 postulates

anchor.snippet: the 4 + 1 Euclid's postulates of plane geometry

annotations.note: Euclid's postulates are not formalized as an axiom system in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Independence of the parallel postulate

anchor.snippet: the fifth postulate was found to be independent of the first four

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize Euclid's postulates nor any independence result for the parallel postulate.

annotations.label: Characterization of the real numbers

anchor.snippet: The real numbers are uniquely picked out (up to isomorphism ) by the properties of a Dedekind complete ordered field

annotations.note: Mathlib proves any two conditionally complete linear ordered fields are uniquely order-ring isomorphic, characterizing ℝ up to unique isomorphism.

annotations.label: Löwenheim–Skolem theorems

anchor.snippet: any axiom system for the reals admits other models

annotations.note: Both the upward (this decl) and downward (`exists_elementarySubstructure_card_eq`) Löwenheim–Skolem theorems are formalized.

annotations.label: Deductive system

anchor.snippet: A deductive system consists of a set

annotations.note: Mathlib's ModelTheory provides semantics (⊨) but no syntactic deductive/proof system.

annotations.label: Completeness of a deductive system

anchor.snippet: A system is said to be complete if, for all formulas

annotations.note: Without a proof calculus this notion is absent; Mathlib's `Theory.IsComplete` is a distinct, purely semantic notion of theory-completeness.

annotations.label: Gödel's completeness theorem

anchor.snippet: Gödel's completeness theorem establishes the completeness of a certain commonly used type of deductive system

annotations.note: Mathlib has the Compactness Theorem but no syntactic provability relation, so Gödel's completeness theorem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Gödel's first incompleteness theorem

anchor.snippet: no recursive , consistent set of non-logical axioms

annotations.note: Only the Gödel Beta Function Lemma, an explicit step toward the First Incompleteness Theorem, is present; the theorem itself is not proved.

annotations.label: Axioms in the modern view

anchor.snippet: axioms may be any set of formulas, as long as they are not known to be inconsistent

annotations.note: A `Theory` captures 'any set of formulas' but the attached consistency caveat relies on `IsSatisfiable`, which is only the semantic analogue of non-inconsistency.
